blocks.text: Joseph Wallace
Chief Financial Officer
Carroll, Merritt and Williams
Re: A program to hold and grow the customers you already have
Dear Mr. Wallace:

This letter records the work you have asked of us, sets out how we will carry it out, and states plainly what it costs and where the risk falls between us. Most of it you and I have already covered in conversation, so read it as a record of what we agreed rather than as anything new. If a term here reads differently from your recollection, telephone me before you sign and we will correct it.
The Problem We Are Taking On

You are losing more of your customers than the business can afford to, and the accounts you keep are worth less to you over time than they once were. You want to understand why, and you want a program you can run afterward without us. This engagement is built to give you both. It answers where and why customers leave, and it hands you a working way to hold and grow the ones you have. The analysis proceeds along four lines:

items: A retention baseline: how many customers and how much revenue you keep and lose, measured by segment and by the year a customer first bought, on a single definition we agree at the outset so that every later figure ties back to it.
The reasons behind the numbers: what your lost and at-risk accounts tell us directly, and what your own service, renewal, and billing records say about the moments when a relationship starts to slip.
The commercial levers: how renewal pricing, discounting, contract terms, and service levels bear on whether a customer stays, and what each of them costs you to pull.
A program you can operate: who owns retention, the signals that flag an account before it leaves, the actions to take when they fire, and a sensible order for putting the whole thing in place.

blocks.text: Let me be as clear about what this engagement is not, so that nothing you are expecting is left unsaid. We do not rebuild your billing or CRM systems, and we do not install software; where a recommendation needs a system change, we describe it and leave the build to the people who own that system. We do not audit your records, and we take the customer and revenue figures your people give us as the record. And we recommend; the decision to act, and the running of the program once it is designed, stay with your managers.
How We Will Work

We work from your own data rather than from industry averages, and every figure in anything we hand you ties back to a workpaper we can produce on request. We keep what we find apart from what we advise, and label each, because a finding and a recommendation do not carry the same weight. We bring you our thinking as it firms up instead of saving it for a closing presentation, so that nothing in the final material reaches you cold. Where a number is still provisional, we mark it provisional and tell you what would settle it.
The People on the Work

Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager, runs the work day to day and is your point of contact for anything to do with progress, scheduling, or data; if you have a question and are unsure whom to ask, ask her. Mary Parker, Research Associate, builds the retention baseline and keeps the underlying records straight, and she is the person to reach on how a figure was derived. I set the direction of the work and read everything before it comes to you. On your side, we will treat you, Joseph Wallace, as our sponsor and the person who clears access and decisions, unless you name someone to stand in that place.
Fee and Start

Our fee to {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} is {{fact:f:E-2019-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope set out above. We expect to begin on {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}} and to reach a completed program design roughly nine months from that date. If you ask us to widen the scope, we will price the addition in writing and will not start it until you have agreed that price. I prefer a fixed fee here so that your people can work alongside us without watching a meter.
Invoicing and Expenses

We bill the fixed fee in equal monthly installments across the term, each invoice payable within thirty days of its date. Travel to your sites and other out-of-pocket costs are billed at cost, itemized, and not marked up.
Duration, and Ending It Early

The engagement runs from the start date above until we deliver the program design, unless one of us ends it sooner. Either of us may end it on fifteen days' written notice, for any reason or none. If you end it early, you owe the fee earned to that date, prorated against the fixed fee, together with expenses already incurred, and we hand over our work in whatever state it has reached.
Confidentiality

Who your customers are, what they pay, and which of them you are at risk of losing are among the most sensitive facts the company holds, and we treat them that way. We use what you give us only for this engagement and disclose it outside our firm only as you direct in writing or as the law requires. We ask the same of you as to our methods, our workpapers, and the fee recorded in this letter.
The Limit of Our Liability

Our total liability arising out of this engagement, however it is framed, is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it, and neither of us will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, lost profits among them. That limit does not reach our own fraud or willful misconduct. Our deliverables are prepared for your internal use; if you mean to put one in front of a lender, a buyer, or anyone else who might rely on it, raise it with me first so that we can deal with the reliance in writing.
Governing Law

This letter is governed by the law of the state in which our principal office sits, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Any dispute we cannot settle between us goes to mediation before either of us turns to a court.

If these terms are acceptable, please countersign both copies and return one to me; the other is yours. We are glad to take on this work, and Deborah Jimenez and Mary Parker are ready to begin on the date set out above.
Sincerely,

To: Engagement team
From: Deborah Jimenez
Re: Kickoff, Carroll, Merritt and Williams customer retention program

Work begins {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}. This memo sets what we are here to do and how the first weeks run. The client wants to know why it loses customers and wants a retention program it can run without us. Everything we produce serves those two ends. We hold to one definition of a lost customer and one of revenue at risk, agreed with the client in the first week, so that every number we report later reconciles to the same base.
The four lines of work

items: Retention baseline. Mary Parker builds the customer and revenue picture by segment and by cohort, from the billing and contract history. This is the spine; the other three read against it.
Reasons for loss. Interviews with lost and at-risk accounts, read alongside the service, renewal, and billing records, to place why customers leave against when they leave.
Commercial levers. How renewal pricing, discounts, contract terms, and service levels bear on retention, and what each costs to move.
Program design. The operating model: who owns retention, the early signals, the actions when they fire, and the order of standing it up.

blocks.text: One risk is visible already. The customer records sit across billing and the CRM, and the two do not always agree on when an account left or what it was worth. If we build the baseline on a source the client's own people distrust, the findings will not hold. So Mary Parker reconciles the two sources first and has the client sign off on the reconciled base before any finding rests on it. Nothing else starts until that base is agreed.

A second risk is access. The people we most need to interview, the account managers who hold the mid-sized accounts, have the least free time. If we chase them one at a time, the interview phase slips. So we book the full set through the sponsor's office in a single pass, with a short fixed set of questions, so that each conversation runs to about thirty minutes and no one is asked back.

Joseph Wallace is our sponsor and our single point of contact for access and decisions. Data requests and interview scheduling route through his office. I will keep the request log for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} current and circulate it weekly so we all see what is outstanding on their side.

Two cadences run through the work. The team reconciles progress against the plan each week, internally. We meet the client at the close of each phase rather than on a fixed date, so that a session has a decision in front of it and not only a status to recite. Between those, the request log carries the running detail.
The first two weeks

items: Agree the definitions of a lost customer and revenue at risk with the client, in writing.
Pull three years of billing, contract, and renewal history, plus the CRM export of account status and reasons for loss.
Reconcile billing against the CRM and flag every account where the two disagree.
Draft the interview list, lost accounts, at-risk accounts, and the account managers who hold them, and clear it with the sponsor's office before anyone is contacted.
Stand up the weekly internal check-in and the request log.

blocks.text: I will send the data request and the interview list to the client this week and confirm dates once their finance and sales staff come back to us. Flag anything that looks wrong before it goes across, not after we have sent it.
Joseph Wallace, copying Mary Parker:

A short status and one request. The baseline is coming together. We have three years of your billing and contract history and the CRM export of account status, and Mary Parker has started reconciling the two so that we count a lost customer the same way your finance and sales teams do. Nothing to report as a finding yet; we are still squaring the two records against each other.

The one gap is the reasons-for-loss field in the CRM. It is filled in for recent accounts and blank for most of the older ones, which is exactly the stretch we need to understand churn on {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}. If anyone on your side kept renewal notes, cancellation letters, or save-desk logs for the accounts that left before this year, those would let us fill it. Even a partial set helps.

Nothing else needed from you today. Once the reconciliation settles, Mary Parker and I will send a short read on what the baseline shows before we walk you through it. The data your team at Carroll, Merritt and Williams has sent so far has come in clean and well labeled, which keeps us on schedule.
Thanks,
Deborah Jimenez
Minutes of the working session on the customer retention program for Carroll, Merritt and Williams, held at the client's offices. Recorded by Mary Parker.
Present

items: Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager
Mary Parker, Research Associate
Joseph Wallace, Chief Financial Officer, {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}

blocks.text: We met to walk Joseph Wallace through where the retention baseline landed and to settle two definitions that had been left open since kickoff. We are far enough in to put firm numbers against who is leaving and what it costs; we are not yet at the point of recommending what to do about it, which Deborah Jimenez said plainly at the start so that nothing today would be read as a conclusion.

I presented the baseline. Measured on the reconciled billing and CRM records, the picture holds together and ties back to the client's own revenue for the period. The losses concentrate in a way the room had half expected: the newest customers leave at a much higher rate than the established ones, and a small number of mid-sized accounts carry a disproportionate share of the revenue at risk. Deborah Jimenez and I kept the confirmed figures apart from the two segments still being cleaned, and nothing discussed today rests on the segments that remain open.

The first definition we settled was what counts as a lost customer. The client's finance team counts an account gone when it is closed in billing; the sales team counts it gone when the account manager marks it dead, which often comes months earlier. We agreed to report both, with the billing definition as the headline and the sales definition as an early read, so that each team sees its own number and the gap between them.

The second was how to treat customers who leave and later return. Joseph Wallace's view was that a customer who comes back within a year was never truly lost, and on the numbers he is mostly right; those returns are common enough to move the rate. We agreed to net them out of the headline churn and to track them on their own, because the win-back pattern is worth understanding in its own right.

We also began the reasons-for-loss work. The interviews with lost accounts are underway, and the early ones point less at price than at service response times on the accounts that left. I flagged this as a working read rather than a finding; we have spoken to only a handful of accounts, and the sample is too thin to lean on. Deborah Jimenez asked that we weight the remaining interviews toward the mid-sized accounts that carry the revenue at risk, and Joseph Wallace agreed to open the account managers' calendars to us.
What we agreed to do

rows: Restate the baseline with the two open segments folded in and returns netted out
Weight the remaining lost-account interviews toward the mid-sized at-risk accounts
Open the account managers' calendars for interviews and confirm the save-desk logs exist
Circulate the two agreed definitions in writing for the finance and sales teams to confirm
Set the next working session once the interviews are largely done

blocks.text:
Customer retention program: status report
Prepared for the executive sponsor, Carroll, Merritt and Williams
Prepared by Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager
Reporting period: the month just ended
Where the program stands

We are about halfway through the program and on schedule. The retention baseline for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} is complete and agreed with your finance and sales teams. The reasons-for-loss work is well advanced, with most of the lost-account interviews done. We have not yet moved to the commercial levers or to the program design; those are the back half of the work. Nothing to date has changed the shape of the engagement or its cost. The two phases that remain are scheduled across the back half and carry no dependency we cannot see today.

Against the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.fee}} for the program, spending tracks the plan: roughly half the fee is consumed at roughly the halfway mark, and we see nothing that would take the work beyond the agreed scope or price. If anything arises that would, you will hear it from me before we act on it.
What we completed this period

items: Closed the retention baseline. The count of customers and revenue kept and lost is final, reconciled to your billing, and signed off by finance and sales on one agreed definition.
Settled the two definitions left open at the last session, what counts as a lost customer and how returning customers are treated, and circulated both for confirmation.
Completed the majority of the lost-account interviews and began sorting the reasons for loss into a small number of recurring causes.
Delivered the at-risk account list: the mid-sized accounts that carry the largest share of revenue at risk, ranked, with the signal that put each on the list.

blocks.text: One early pattern is firm enough to name. On the accounts that have left, service response time separates the leavers from the stayers more sharply than price does. Mary Parker has the figures behind this and carries them into the commercial-lever work, where we test what it would cost to close the response-time gap against what it would save in retained revenue.

A second pattern is worth flagging, though it is less settled. The accounts most at risk are not the smallest ones, as the internal read had assumed, but a band of mid-sized accounts, large enough to matter and small enough to have slipped below the attention the largest accounts get. Whether that is a coverage gap or a pricing one is what the commercial-lever work should tell us; we are not calling it yet.
What comes next

items: Test the commercial levers, renewal pricing, discounting, contract terms, and service levels, against the baseline, and size for each what it costs to move and what it returns in retained revenue.
Turn the at-risk list into a working early-warning indicator your account managers can read week to week, built only from measures that ran ahead of a loss in the historical data.
Draft the retention operating model: who owns retention, the signals that trigger action, the actions themselves, and the order of standing the program up.
Bring a small group of your account managers into the design work, so the program is built with the people who will run it rather than handed to them.

blocks.text: Risks, and what we are doing about them
Two risks are worth your attention, each with a response already in motion.

The first is data on the commercial levers. Renewal pricing and discount history are thinner in your records than the billing data was, and without them the lever analysis rests on judgment rather than evidence. We have asked your commercial team for the renewal and discount records, and Mary Parker is assembling what exists into a usable set. If it comes back too thin, we will tell you plainly which conclusions the data can carry and which it cannot.

The second is adoption. A retention program only works if your account managers run it after we leave, and a design handed down without their input rarely holds. We are bringing a small group of your account managers into the program-design sessions now, so that what we hand you in the back half of the work is something they helped shape.
I am glad to walk through any of this by telephone. The next report will carry the first results from the commercial-lever work.
Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager
Minutes of the working session on the customer retention program for Carroll, Merritt and Williams, held at our offices. Recorded by Mary Parker.
In the room

items: Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager
Mary Parker, Research Associate
Joseph Wallace, Chief Financial Officer, {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}

blocks.text: With the back half of the work well along, this was the first session at which we could put the pieces together for Joseph Wallace and show how the program would actually run. We are close enough to the end to talk about how it gets put in place; we are not there yet, and Deborah Jimenez framed what we showed as a draft the client's own people still need to shape.

I took the room through the results of the commercial-lever work. Of the four levers we tested against the baseline, service response time and renewal timing return the most retained revenue for what they cost; broad discounting returns the least and, on our numbers, mostly gives away margin on customers who would have stayed anyway. Deborah Jimenez and I kept the sizing apart from the recommendation, and I marked where a figure still rests on the thinner renewal-pricing data rather than on the billing records.

Most of the discussion was on the early-warning indicator. We have turned the at-risk list into a weekly signal an account manager can read, built from the handful of measures that ran ahead of a loss in the historical data. Joseph Wallace pressed on whether his managers would trust it, and it was a fair challenge; a score no one believes gets ignored. We agreed to put the indicator in front of a small group of account managers before it is finalized and to let their objections change it.

On the program itself, the draft operating model now names who owns retention, what the account managers do when the signal fires, and the order of standing the whole thing up. Joseph Wallace asked that we not hand it over as a finished document but pilot it on one region first, so that his people learn to run it while we are still here to help. We agreed. Deborah Jimenez and I will scope a single-region pilot for the final stretch of the engagement.

We closed on what the final deliverables will be. Joseph Wallace will receive the program itself, the early-warning indicator with the notes needed to run it, and a short written case that sets the cost of the program against the revenue it is built to retain. Deborah Jimenez asked that the case be conservative, resting on the retained revenue we can defend from the baseline rather than on the fuller figure the levers might support, and Joseph Wallace said he would rather carry a number he could stand behind to his own board than a larger one he could not.
Agreed actions

items: Scope a single-region pilot of the retention program for the final stretch. Owners: Deborah Jimenez and Mary Parker, within two weeks.
Put the early-warning indicator in front of a small group of account managers and revise it against their objections. Owner: Mary Parker, before the pilot is set.
Firm up the renewal-pricing figures where the lever sizing still rests on thin data. Owner: Mary Parker, ongoing.
Confirm which region the pilot runs in and name the account managers who will run it. Owner: Joseph Wallace, within two weeks.
Draft the final report and implementation plan around the pilot rather than ahead of it. Owner: Deborah Jimenez, before the closing session.
